name: Java & DevOps · Core
description: Graduate-level Java & DevOps. Practical, reasoning-based questions.

questions.text: Why do developers usually create feature branches?
options: To automatically delete the branch on push
To make the repository clone much faster
To permanently hide history from teammates
To isolate work before merging into main
questions.explanation: Feature branches let you work without destabilising shared code, and merge only when the change is ready and reviewed.

questions.text: What is the main purpose of a pull request?
options: To delete a branch once it is finished
To push code directly to main without any review
To permanently lock the main branch
To request review and discuss before merging
questions.explanation: A PR is a checkpoint for review, discussion and knowledge sharing before code reaches the main branch.

questions.text: You hit a merge conflict. What is the right approach?
options: Delete the file and commit an empty version
Always keep your own side and discard theirs
Read both changes, resolve deliberately, then test
Force-push straight over the branch to erase the clash
questions.explanation: A conflict means two changes touched the same lines. You have to understand both intents and combine them, not blindly pick a side.

questions.text: You accidentally pushed an API key in a commit. What should you do first?
options: Make the repository private and leave it there
Delete it in a later commit and move on
Rotate and revoke the key; it stays in history
Nothing; Git encrypts committed secrets for you
questions.explanation: The secret is compromised the moment it's pushed and remains in history. Rotating it is the only real fix; scrubbing history is secondary.

questions.text: Why prefer small, frequent commits over one huge commit?
options: They are easier to review, revert and bisect
They make the repository clone much faster
They use noticeably less storage on the server
Git refuses to accept overly large commits
questions.explanation: Small commits make reviews meaningful and let you pinpoint or undo a specific change instead of an all-or-nothing blob.

questions.text: On a shared branch others have pulled, which is safer to undo a bad commit?
options: git commit --amend on the pushed commit
git reset --hard to erase it from the branch
git revert, which adds a reversing commit
Delete the branch and recreate it fresh
questions.explanation: revert adds a new commit that undoes the change without rewriting shared history, so it won't break everyone else's clones.

questions.text: What belongs in .gitignore?
options: The main application source code files
Build output, local config and secrets
The project's README and documentation
Every file the project needs to compile
questions.explanation: Generated artefacts and secrets shouldn't be versioned. Keeping them out avoids noise and accidental credential leaks.

questions.text: Why review a diff before committing?
options: To catch debug code, secrets and stray changes
To make the commit upload a little faster
Because Git refuses to commit a diff nobody has read
To automatically format the changed lines
questions.explanation: A quick self-review before committing catches leftover debugging, stray files and secrets before they enter history.

questions.text: In a CI/CD pipeline, what is the main benefit of failing early on tests?
options: Developers can safely skip writing more tests
The pipeline uses far less disk space overall
Bugs are discovered sooner, when cheap to fix
The final build artifact comes out smaller
questions.explanation: Fast failure gives quick feedback so problems are fixed before they reach later stages or production.

questions.text: A build works locally but fails in the pipeline. What should you check first?
options: The number of branches currently in the repo
The colour scheme of the pipeline dashboard
Whether the repository has accumulated enough total stars
Env vars, tool versions, missing files, OS differences
questions.explanation: 'Works on my machine' failures almost always come from differences between your laptop and the clean CI environment.

questions.text: Why automate builds and deployments instead of doing them by hand?
options: It makes the compiled binary itself run much faster
It removes the need for any tests at all
Repeatable, consistent results with fewer errors
It lets you skip code review entirely
questions.explanation: Automation makes the process deterministic and auditable, removing the mistakes that come with manual steps.

questions.text: A test fails intermittently in CI. What's the healthiest response?
options: Disable all tests until the release ships
Delete the test so the pipeline stays green
Re-run the pipeline repeatedly until it happens to pass
Investigate and fix it, or quarantine it clearly
questions.explanation: Flaky tests erode trust in the pipeline. Blindly re-running hides real bugs; fix the cause or isolate it explicitly.

questions.text: Why keep pipeline runs fast?
options: It reduces the storage the pipeline consumes
Slow pipelines make the artifact more reliable
Speed has no real effect on the developers
Fast feedback keeps developers productive
questions.explanation: Slow pipelines discourage frequent integration and delay feedback, which is where most of CI's value comes from.

questions.text: Best place to store deploy credentials used by the pipeline?
options: In the CI/CD secrets store, injected at runtime
Hard-coded directly inside the pipeline script
Committed to the repository in a plain file
Emailed to whoever happens to run the deploy
questions.explanation: Secrets belong in a managed store and are injected as needed, never committed where anyone with repo access can read them.

questions.text: Why build an artifact once and promote the same one through environments?
options: To guarantee what you tested is what you ship
To make each environment build a bit faster
Because rebuilding per stage is not allowed
To reduce the number of branches you need
questions.explanation: Rebuilding per environment risks subtle differences. Promoting one immutable artifact means staging and prod run identical bits.

questions.text: What is a Docker container?
options: A full virtual machine with its own kernel
A lightweight isolated environment for an app
A remote server that hosts your source code
A registry that stores built container images
questions.explanation: A container packages an app with its dependencies so it runs consistently across machines and environments.

questions.text: Why are environment variables commonly used for configuration?
options: Because code cannot read values from a file
To make the application start up much faster
To keep configuration outside the source code
To encrypt the configuration automatically
questions.explanation: Values that differ per environment (URLs, keys, flags) live outside the code so the same build runs everywhere.

questions.text: What's the difference between a Docker image and a container?
options: An image runs while a container is just stored
A container is the template; an image runs it
They are two names for the exact same thing
An image is the template; a container runs it
questions.explanation: An image is the immutable blueprint; a container is a live, running instance created from that image.

questions.text: Your Docker image is huge and slow to pull. A good first step?
options: Use a smaller base image or a multi-stage build
Add more image layers to spread out the download
Pull the image over a faster network link
Run the container with far more memory
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds drop build-time tooling from the final image, and a slimmer base cuts size directly.

questions.text: Why is baking secrets into an image a bad idea?
options: The build simply takes much longer to finish
It makes the image considerably larger on disk
Anyone who can pull the image can read them
Docker refuses to run an image holding secrets
questions.explanation: Image layers are readable by anyone with access to the image, so embedded secrets are effectively published.

questions.text: A container exits immediately after starting. What do you check first?
options: How many other containers are running on the same host
The colour of the terminal running it
The container logs and the main process's exit
The total number of images in the registry
questions.explanation: The logs usually show why the main process died; a container stops as soon as its main process exits.

questions.text: Why run a container process as a non-root user when possible?
options: It reduces the final size of the image on disk
It makes the container start up much faster
Root is not permitted inside any container
Least privilege limits damage if it's compromised
questions.explanation: Running as non-root follows least privilege, so a compromised process can't freely affect the host or other workloads.

questions.text: What is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?
options: A container
A pod
A node
A service
questions.explanation: A pod wraps one or more tightly-coupled containers and is the unit Kubernetes schedules and manages.

questions.text: What is a readiness probe for?
options: Restarting a pod that has become stuck
Deciding when a pod should receive traffic
Scaling the number of replicas up or down
Choosing which node a pod is placed on
questions.explanation: Readiness gates traffic: a pod is kept out of the load-balancer rotation until it reports ready.

questions.text: Liveness vs readiness probe — key difference?
options: Both do exactly the same thing to the pod
Readiness restarts a stuck one; liveness gates traffic
Liveness restarts a stuck one; readiness gates traffic
Neither affects live traffic routing at all
questions.explanation: A failing liveness probe restarts the container; a failing readiness probe just removes it from serving traffic.

questions.text: A pod is in CrashLoopBackOff. What do you look at first?
options: The colour shown next to the pod in the UI
The pod's current and previous container logs
How many nodes the cluster currently has
The total number of pods across all namespaces
questions.explanation: CrashLoopBackOff means the container keeps dying; the logs (including the previous crashed instance) usually show why.

questions.text: A new rollout broke production. Quickest safe action?
options: Roll back to the previous known-good revision
Delete the whole deployment and start over
Restart every node in the cluster at once
Scale the broken version up to more replicas
questions.explanation: Restore service first by rolling back, then diagnose the bad release calmly out of the critical path.

questions.text: Where should a database password live in Kubernetes?
options: In a ConfigMap
In a Secret
In the container image
In a plain environment file
questions.explanation: Secrets are the intended place for sensitive values; ConfigMaps are for non-confidential configuration.

questions.text: Risk of running pods with no resource requests/limits?
options: One workload can starve the others on the node
Pods will simply refuse to start at all
The scheduler places every pod on one node
Kubernetes automatically doubles the limits
questions.explanation: Without limits a noisy pod can consume a node's CPU/memory and degrade everything else scheduled there.

questions.text: A stateless web service is overloaded. Simplest scaling move?
options: Restart the pod repeatedly until it copes
Give the single pod a much bigger CPU
Add more replicas behind the service
Move the pod onto a slightly faster node
questions.explanation: Stateless services scale horizontally: more identical replicas share the load behind one service endpoint.

questions.text: Why does Kubernetes use declarative manifests?
options: You describe desired state and it reconciles
You list each command to run, step by step
You must apply changes manually every time
It only works for stateless applications
questions.explanation: You declare what you want; the control loop continuously works to make reality match that desired state.

questions.text: Why run 'terraform plan' before 'apply'?
options: To review exactly what will change beforehand
To make the following apply run much faster
Because apply needs a plan file to exist
To automatically format the Terraform code
questions.explanation: plan is a dry run showing adds/changes/destroys, so you catch surprises before touching real infrastructure.

questions.text: Why shouldn't you hand-edit Terraform state?
options: Editing it is fine and the usual way to fix things
The state file is far too large to open safely
Terraform encrypts the state so you can't read it
It maps config to real resources; edits cause drift
questions.explanation: State is the source of truth linking code to real resources. Editing it by hand easily desyncs Terraform from reality.

questions.text: Someone changed a resource in the AWS console that Terraform manages. What happens?
options: Terraform deletes the resource on the next apply
Drift: the next plan wants to revert it to code
Nothing at all; Terraform never notices it
The state file is corrupted and must be rebuilt
questions.explanation: Terraform compares real state to code; out-of-band changes show as drift and will be reconciled on the next apply.

questions.text: Main reason to use Infrastructure as Code over clicking in a console?
options: Clicking in a console cannot create resources
It always provisions resources much more quickly
It removes the need to understand the cloud
Repeatable, reviewable, version-controlled infra
questions.explanation: IaC makes infra changes auditable and reproducible, and lets you review them like any other code.

questions.text: You need a secret value in Terraform. What do you avoid?
options: Marking the variable as sensitive in config
Passing it in through a variable at apply time
Storing it in a dedicated secrets manager
Committing it in plain text inside the repo
questions.explanation: Never commit secrets in code or tfvars; use a secrets manager or injected variables, and mark outputs sensitive.

questions.text: An app is running but users report errors. What do you check first?
options: The number of commits made in the last week
The colour theme used on the application login page
How many total users are registered in it
Logs, recent deploys, health, config, dependencies
questions.explanation: Production issues need runtime evidence first: logs, recent changes, health checks, config and external dependencies.

questions.text: Why prefer structured (e.g. JSON) logs over free-form text?
options: Free-form text cannot be written to a file
They take up considerably less disk space than text
They make the application itself run faster
They're far easier to search, filter and aggregate
questions.explanation: Structured fields let tools query and correlate logs reliably instead of fragile text parsing.

questions.text: You need to follow a single request across several services. What helps most?
options: A longer request timeout
A single combined log file
Distributed tracing
More CPU on each service
questions.explanation: Traces stitch together a request's path across services, which metrics and individual logs alone can't show.

questions.text: First thing to do when a real incident starts?
options: Blame whoever made the most recent commit
Immediately rewrite the failing component
Wait quietly to see if it resolves itself
Assess impact and severity, then communicate
questions.explanation: Understanding blast radius and telling stakeholders comes first; coordinated response beats silent lone debugging.

questions.text: A non-critical alert pages the team every night. Best response?
options: Ignore the pages and hope it stops eventually
Delete the monitoring for that system entirely
Tune the threshold so the alert is actionable
Page a larger group so someone always answers
questions.explanation: Noisy alerts cause fatigue and missed real ones. Alerts should be actionable; tune or remove the noise.

questions.text: Production is down after a deploy and the cause is unclear. Usual first move?
options: Scale the broken deploy up to more replicas
Debug live in production before restoring it
Wait for the next scheduled release to fix it
Roll back to restore service, then investigate
questions.explanation: Under an outage, restoring service (rollback) usually beats debugging forward while users are affected.

questions.text: Why do health checks matter for a platform like Kubernetes or a load balancer?
options: They reduce the memory each instance uses
They make each request noticeably faster
They encrypt all of the traffic reaching the instances
They let the platform replace unhealthy instances
questions.explanation: Health checks give the platform a signal to stop routing to, restart, or replace instances that aren't working.

questions.text: A request returns HTTP 500. Where do you look?
options: The user's browser console and cookies
Server-side logs — it is a server error
The DNS records for the application domain
The client's local network configuration
questions.explanation: 5xx means the server failed to handle a valid request, so the evidence is in server-side logs and code.

questions.text: Behind a reverse proxy you get 502/504 errors. Likely area?
options: The user typing the wrong URL in the browser
The upstream backend being down or unreachable
A missing favicon on the front-end site
Too many CSS files loading on the page
questions.explanation: 502/504 mean the proxy couldn't get a good/timely response from the backend, so investigate the upstream service.

questions.text: What does the principle of least privilege mean?
options: Grant broad access now and trim it back later
Give everyone admin so nothing ever blocks
Grant only the access that is actually needed
Share one powerful account across the team
questions.explanation: Minimising granted permissions limits the damage from mistakes or compromised credentials.

questions.text: Why externalise configuration per environment (12-factor style)?
options: Because code cannot read from a config file
So each environment can run its own different build
So one build runs unchanged across all environments
To make the application start up much faster
questions.explanation: Config in the environment (not the code) lets the identical artifact behave correctly everywhere.

questions.text: What does a load balancer primarily do?
options: Compile the application before it is deployed
Store the application's files and serve them
Encrypt every request that reaches the backend
Distribute traffic across instances for scale
questions.explanation: It spreads requests across healthy backends, improving throughput and tolerating individual instance failures.

questions.text: Why is it valuable for a deploy/operation to be idempotent?
options: Running it again always doubles what it did
Running it again safely yields the same state
It can only ever be run one single time
It runs noticeably faster on the second run
questions.explanation: Idempotent operations can be retried without harm, which is essential for reliable automation and recovery.

questions.text: What happens if you call .equals() on a variable that is null?
options: Returns true because both sides are null
Quietly returns false without any error
Throws a NullPointerException
Fails to compile before it can even run
questions.explanation: You can't call a method on null. Prefer "literal".equals(var) or Objects.equals(a, b) to stay null-safe.

questions.text: Difference between == and .equals() for objects?
options: equals only works on primitive number types
== compares values; equals compares references
They behave identically for all object types
== compares references; equals compares values
questions.explanation: == asks 'same object?'; .equals() can be overridden to ask 'logically equal?'. Confusing them is a classic bug.

questions.text: Safest way to compare two possibly-null objects for equality?
options: b.equals(a) directly
a.equals(b) directly
a == b by reference identity
Objects.equals(a, b)
questions.explanation: Objects.equals handles nulls for you, avoiding an NPE when the first reference is null.

questions.text: If you override equals(), what else must you override?
options: toString()
hashCode()
compareTo()
clone()
questions.explanation: Equal objects must have equal hash codes, or HashMap/HashSet will behave incorrectly.

questions.text: Why is comparing strings with == often a bug?
options: == always returns true for two equal strings
== is far slower than calling the equals method
== cannot be used on strings and won't compile
== compares references, not the character contents
questions.explanation: Two strings with the same text can be different objects; use .equals() to compare their contents.

questions.text: Key difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
options: Checked ones must be handled or declared
Unchecked ones must be handled or declared
Both types must always be caught explicitly
Neither kind ever needs to be handled at all
questions.explanation: The compiler enforces checked exceptions; unchecked ones (like NullPointerException) usually signal programming errors.

questions.text: Why is catching an exception and doing nothing dangerous?
options: It automatically retries the failed operation
It makes the program run considerably faster
It is required whenever you catch an exception
It hides failures and makes bugs invisible
questions.explanation: Swallowing exceptions silently loses the error, so problems surface later in confusing ways with no trail.

questions.text: What does try-with-resources give you?
options: Automatic retrying of any code that throws
Automatic closing of resources, avoiding leaks
A guaranteed speed-up for file operations
Silent swallowing of every exception thrown
questions.explanation: Resources declared in try-with-resources are closed automatically, even on exceptions, preventing leaks.

questions.text: A method can't produce a valid result. Better than returning null?
options: Return the number minus one to signal failure
Return an empty string in every failure case
Throw a clear exception or return an Optional
Print an error message and then return null
questions.explanation: Signalling absence explicitly (exception or Optional) prevents silent nulls that blow up far from the cause.

questions.text: Why catch specific exception types rather than Exception?
options: So you handle each failure appropriately
So the program always runs a little faster
Because catching Exception fails to compile
So every exception is silently ignored
questions.explanation: Catching broadly can swallow unexpected errors; specific catches let you react correctly and let others propagate.

questions.text: What is the finally block for?
options: Cleanup that runs whether or not it threw
Code that runs only when no exception occurs
Code that runs only when an exception occurs
A faster alternative to a normal catch block
questions.explanation: finally always executes, making it the place for cleanup that must happen regardless of the outcome.

questions.text: Which collection does not allow duplicate elements?
options: Set
List
Map
Queue
questions.explanation: A Set stores unique elements; a List allows duplicates and a Map stores key-value pairs.

questions.text: You need ordered items and duplicates are allowed. Which fits?
options: Map
Set
List
HashSet
questions.explanation: A List keeps insertion order and permits duplicates, which a Set does not.

questions.text: You look values up by a unique key. Which fits best?
options: Map
List
Set
Array
questions.explanation: A Map is designed for key-to-value lookups, far cheaper than scanning a List.

questions.text: Many random-access reads by index vs frequent inserts in the middle — ArrayList vs LinkedList?
options: LinkedList for random access; ArrayList for inserts
ArrayList for random access; LinkedList for inserts
Both perform identically for every operation
ArrayList cannot be indexed by position at all
questions.explanation: ArrayList gives O(1) indexed access; LinkedList avoids shifting on inserts/removes but is slow to index.

questions.text: You modify a list while iterating it with a for-each loop. Likely result?
options: The loop simply skips the changed element
The list is silently sorted as you go
A ConcurrentModificationException
Nothing; modifying during iteration is fine
questions.explanation: Structural changes during iteration throw ConcurrentModificationException; use an Iterator's remove or a copy.

questions.text: What makes a good HashMap key?
options: A mutable object whose fields change over time
An immutable object with stable equals/hashCode
Any object, since keys are copied on insert
Only a plain String may ever be used as a key
questions.explanation: If a key's hashCode changes after insertion, you can't find it again; immutable keys keep lookups reliable.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an interface in Java?
options: To define a contract that classes implement
To store the shared state for its subclasses
To run code faster than a normal class would
To prevent a class from ever being extended
questions.explanation: An interface specifies what methods a type provides, decoupling callers from any particular implementation.

questions.text: When is an interface preferable to an abstract class?
options: When you want to store mutable instance state
When you need to share fields between classes
When only one class will ever implement it
When you need a contract with no shared state
questions.explanation: Interfaces express capabilities without shared state; abstract classes suit shared code/state and single inheritance.

questions.text: Why is composition often preferred over inheritance?
options: It removes the need to write or define any interfaces
It is always faster than using inheritance
It's more flexible and avoids fragile hierarchies
Inheritance simply cannot be used in Java
questions.explanation: Composing behaviour keeps designs flexible and avoids tightly-coupled hierarchies that are hard to change.

questions.text: Main reason to use dependency injection?
options: It makes the application start up much faster
Loose coupling and easier testing with fakes
It removes the need to write any constructors
It automatically validates all of your inputs
questions.explanation: Passing dependencies in lets you substitute test doubles and change implementations without editing the class.

questions.text: What does encapsulation give you?
options: Running several methods at the same time
Copying one object's fields into another object verbatim
Letting any class read and change every field
Hiding internal state behind a controlled interface
questions.explanation: By keeping fields private and exposing behaviour, you can change internals without breaking callers.

questions.text: Why mark a field final when it shouldn't change?
options: It encrypts the field's value while in memory
It makes the field load much faster at runtime
It shows intent and prevents accidental reassignment
It allows the field to be freely changed from anywhere
questions.explanation: final documents immutability and lets the compiler catch accidental writes, aiding correctness and thread-safety.

questions.text: Why avoid making everything static?
options: It creates global state that's hard to test
Static fields simply cannot be read at runtime
It makes the whole program run much slower
Static methods are not allowed to be called
questions.explanation: Static state is effectively global, making behaviour order-dependent and awkward to isolate in tests.

questions.text: The Single Responsibility Principle says a class should…
options: Contain as many methods as possible
Have one single reason to change
Never be split into separate classes
Depend on every other class in the system
questions.explanation: Focusing a class on one responsibility keeps it easier to understand, test and change safely.

questions.text: A benefit of immutable objects?
options: They're inherently thread-safe and predictable
They use far less memory than mutable objects
Their fields can still be changed when needed
They are always faster to create than mutable
questions.explanation: If state never changes after construction, objects can be shared across threads without synchronisation.

questions.text: What is Optional designed to express?
options: The possible absence of a value, instead of null
A value that is guaranteed to always be present
A collection holding many values at once
A number that may be positive or negative
questions.explanation: Optional makes 'maybe no value' explicit in the type, nudging callers to handle the empty case.

questions.text: Where is Optional NOT intended to be used?
options: To avoid returning a bare null reference
As the return type of a lookup that may miss
To signal that a method might find nothing
As fields and method parameters everywhere
questions.explanation: Optional is meant for return values; using it for fields/params adds overhead and awkwardness without much gain.

questions.text: Streams vs classic loops — a fair statement?
options: Streams often read better but aren't always faster
Streams are always strictly faster than equivalent loops
Loops cannot express the same transformations
Streams and loops are identical in every way
questions.explanation: Choose streams for clarity on data pipelines; performance depends on the case, not on syntax alone.

questions.text: What does 'var' (local variable type inference) change?
options: Reduces boilerplate; the type is still static
Makes the variable's type change at runtime
Turns Java into a dynamically typed language
Lets a variable hold any type interchangeably
questions.explanation: var infers the compile-time type from the initializer; the variable remains strongly, statically typed.

questions.text: Two threads update the same mutable counter without coordination. Risk?
options: A deadlock the first time both threads run
A compile error before the program can run
A race condition producing lost updates
Nothing; the counter stays perfectly correct
questions.explanation: Unsynchronised access to shared mutable state can interleave and lose updates; you need synchronisation or atomics.

questions.text: What causes a race condition?
options: Outcome depending on unsynchronised timing
Running too many threads on a single core
A method that is declared as being final
Using an immutable object across threads
questions.explanation: When correctness depends on the interleaving of threads over shared state, results become nondeterministic.

questions.text: Why does immutability help with concurrency?
options: Immutable data is automatically synchronised
Immutable objects run faster on every CPU
Immutable data can't be corrupted by writes
It lets threads change shared state safely
questions.explanation: If nothing can mutate an object, multiple threads can read it safely with no locking.

questions.text: What typically causes a deadlock?
options: An exception that is caught but never logged anywhere
A single thread running for far too long
Too many objects being created at once
Threads waiting on each other's locks in a cycle
questions.explanation: Circular lock dependencies mean each thread holds what another needs, so none can proceed.

questions.text: What does 'volatile' guarantee?
options: That only one thread may ever read the field
That the whole operation is fully atomic
Visibility of the latest value across threads
A large speed-up for every field access
questions.explanation: volatile ensures reads see the latest write, but it does not make compound actions like i++ atomic.

questions.text: Building a big string by concatenating in a loop — better approach?
options: Add the pieces with the + operator
Use a StringBuilder
Store each piece in a separate variable
Concatenate them all on a single line
questions.explanation: String is immutable, so += in a loop creates many objects; StringBuilder appends in place efficiently.

questions.text: You suspect a performance problem. First step?
options: Rewrite the slowest-looking method by guess
Measure and profile to find the real bottleneck
Add more servers before looking at anything
Switch to a different programming language
questions.explanation: Optimise based on evidence, not guesses; profiling shows where time is actually spent.

questions.text: Autoboxing inside a hot loop can hurt because…
options: It makes the loop silently skip some of its iterations
It converts the numbers into text each pass
It silently creates wrapper objects, adding GC pressure
It has no measurable effect on performance
questions.explanation: Repeated boxing of primitives to wrappers allocates objects you didn't intend, which can dominate a tight loop.

questions.text: Your code calls a service once per item in a large loop. Concern?
options: The loop will simply refuse to compile
Many round-trips (N+1) — consider batching
Each call makes the next one run faster
Nothing; per-item calls are always ideal
questions.explanation: Per-item remote calls multiply latency; batching or a bulk API drastically cuts total round-trips.

questions.text: Good logging practice?
options: Use appropriate levels and never log secrets
Log everything, passwords included, at all times
Only ever log at the single highest level
Write every log line to standard output only
questions.explanation: Sensible levels keep logs useful, and secrets must never be written to logs regardless of level.

questions.text: Main value of unit tests?
options: A replacement for ever reading the code again
A guarantee the code has no bugs whatsoever
Fast feedback and protection against regressions
Slower delivery in exchange for nicer reports
questions.explanation: Unit tests catch breakages quickly as code changes, giving confidence to refactor and ship.

questions.text: Unit test vs integration test — the difference?
options: Integration tests a piece alone; unit tests together
Unit tests a piece alone; integration tests together
They are two names for the same kind of test
Unit tests can only ever be run in production
questions.explanation: Unit tests isolate one unit; integration tests verify that multiple parts interact correctly.

questions.text: A single test asserts ten unrelated things and fails. Problem?
options: It is the ideal way to keep the suite small
Hard to tell what broke; test one behaviour each
The test will always pass no matter what
Ten assertions make the test run much faster
questions.explanation: Focused tests pinpoint failures; a sprawling test tells you something broke but not what.

questions.text: Which inputs most often reveal bugs and deserve tests?
options: Only the typical, everyday happy-path values and flows
Edge cases: null, empty, boundaries, invalid input
Whatever inputs are quickest to type by hand
Inputs that are already known to work fine
questions.explanation: Bugs cluster at boundaries and unusual inputs; happy-path-only tests miss the risky cases.

questions.text: Why do code review beyond catching bugs?
options: It is the only way to run the test suite
It spreads knowledge and improves consistency
It makes the merged code run measurably faster
It removes the need to write any documentation
questions.explanation: Review shares context across the team and keeps the codebase coherent, not just bug-free.

questions.text: Why replace 'magic numbers' with named constants?
options: It reduces the size of the compiled binary
Named constants make the program run faster
Numbers simply cannot appear in the source
Names explain intent and centralise changes
questions.explanation: A named constant documents what the value means and lets you change it in one place.

questions.text: Why do meaningful variable and method names matter?
options: Short cryptic names make the program run faster
Code is read far more than written; names aid it
The compiler rejects names over eight letters
Names have no real effect on maintainability
questions.explanation: Readable names reduce the effort to understand and safely change code later.

questions.text: You find unused (dead) code. What's usually best?
options: Leave it in place in case it is needed one day
Remove it — version control remembers it anyway
Comment it out and keep it there indefinitely
Copy it into every file so nothing is lost
questions.explanation: Dead code adds noise and confusion; Git history preserves it if you ever need it back.

questions.text: How should you treat input coming from users or external systems?
options: Validate it before you use it anywhere
Trust it, since the sender has already checked
Log it verbatim and then use it directly
Pass it straight into a query without checks
questions.explanation: External input is untrusted; validating it prevents crashes and security issues like injection.

questions.text: Reading a Java stack trace, where do you usually start?
options: The top exception and the first line in your code
The very last line, regardless of its content
The longest line found anywhere in the trace
A line chosen at random from the middle
questions.explanation: The top shows the exception; scanning down to the first frame in your code usually points at the cause.

questions.text: Why deploy images by an explicit version tag rather than ':latest' in production?
options: You get a known, reproducible version to ship
The image will always download much faster
':latest' cannot be used in a production cluster
Explicit tags make the container use less memory
questions.explanation: ':latest' can point at different images over time, so a pinned version keeps deploys reproducible and rollbacks meaningful.
